Patch Set 5:

After investigation, I would tend to think the problem seen with the in-kernel fscrypt library is limited to file migration (sanity-sec test_52 and test_59b). In this use case, Lustre creates a temporary, volatile file to migrate data. For encrypted files, we explicitly set the encryption context of the volatile file (directly on server side) to be the same as the original file, so that data is encrypted with the same key. Problem is Lustre stores the encryption context in the "security.c" xattr, whereas fscrypt expects it to be in "encryption.c". When using the embedded llcrypt lib, it is fine as all parties use "security.c".

So I have rebased this patch on top of the latest encryption related patches on master, to see if using "encryption.c" xattr makes in-kernel fscrypt happy.

Patch Set 7:

After _deeper_ investigation, it turned out the encryption context of the original file (the one being migrated) was simply not retrieved. So the volatile file had its own, which made the migrated content encrypted with a different key. And this key was trashed. As a consequence, the content of the file after migration could not just be decrypted properly.

parents.subject: LU-15420 sec: handle simple fscrypt changes for 5.15 kernels

commit.subject: LU-13717 sec: test native fscrypt for Ubunutu 5.4 kernel
commit.message:
LU-13717 sec: test native fscrypt for Ubunutu 5.4 kernel

Ensure fscrypt for lustre works as expected on at least Ubuntu 5.4
kernels.

Change-Id: I28caaa57f3c7c479ae192716aed3fbddae700f69
Test-Parameters: fortestonly
Test-Parameters: clientdistro=ubuntu2004 serverdistro=el8.4 trivial testlist=sanity-sec
Signed-off-by: James Simmons <jsimmons@infradead.org>
